was a [constituency](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") from 1918 to 1974 and again from 1983 to 2024.[[a]](#cite_note-FPTP-3) Its final [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) was [Paul Howell](/wiki/Paul_Howell_(MP) "Paul Howell (MP)") of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), who held the seat from [2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") until its abolition for the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

**Sedgefield** was a [constituency](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") from 1918 to 1974 and again from 1983 to 2024.[[a]](#cite_note-FPTP-3) Its final [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) was [Paul Howell](/wiki/Paul_Howell_(MP) "Paul Howell (MP)") of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), who held the seat from [2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") until its abolition for the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

From 1983 to 2007, its MP was [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair"), who served as Prime Minister from 1997 to 2007.

Under the [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies"), the seat was abolished. Subject to major boundary changes, though retaining the town of [Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield "Sedgefield"), the constituency was reformed as [Newton Aycliffe and Spennymoor](/wiki/Newton_Aycliffe_and_Spennymoor "Newton Aycliffe and Spennymoor") to reflect the two largest communities in the revised seat.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1918–1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1918–1974")]

Sedgefield was first created under the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") for the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"), comprising primarily southern parts of the abolished [South Eastern Division of Durham](/wiki/South_East_Durham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South East Durham (UK Parliament constituency)"), including the communities of [Segefield](/wiki/Sedgefield "Sedgefield") and [Billingham](/wiki/Billingham "Billingham"). It also included parts of the former [Mid Durham](/wiki/Mid_Durham "Mid Durham") seat ([Ferryhill](/wiki/Ferryhill "Ferryhill")) and a small area transferred from [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland") ([Chilton](/wiki/Chilton,_County_Durham "Chilton, County Durham")).

It was abolished for the [February 1974 general election](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election"), when its contents were distributed to the neighbouring seats of [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Bishop Auckland (UK Parliament constituency)") (Darlington RD), [Durham](/wiki/City_of_Durham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "City of Durham (UK Parliament constituency)") (Sedgefield RD), [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") (Stockton RD) and [Teesside, Stockton](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stockton-on-Tees (UK Parliament constituency)") (Billingham UD).

### 1983–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1983–present")]

The constituency was recreated at the next redistribution, which came into effect at the [1983 general election](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election"), with similar boundaries, but excluding [Billingham](/wiki/Billingham "Billingham") and [Newton Aycliffe](/wiki/Newton_Aycliffe "Newton Aycliffe") and including [Spennymoor](/wiki/Spennymoor "Spennymoor").

## Boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Boundaries")]

### 1918–1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1918–1950")]

* The Rural Districts of Darlington, Hartlepool, Sedgefield, and Stockton[[4]](#cite_note-5)

### 1950–1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1950–1974")]

* The Urban District of Billingham; and
* the Rural Districts of Darlington, Sedgefield and Stockton.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-6)

*Minor changes – the Rural District of Stockton had been altered, absorbing the Rural District of Hartlepool, but losing Billingham to a new urban district.*

From 1955, the boundaries of the Rural Districts of Darlington, Sedgefield and Stockton were altered in line with changes to local authority boundaries.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

### 1983–1997

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1983–1997")]

* The District of Sedgefield wards of Bishop Middleham, Broom, Chilton, Cornforth, Ferryhill, Fishburn, Low Spennymoor and Tudhoe Grange, Middlestone, New Trimdon and Trimdon Grange, Old Trimdon, Sedgefield, Spennymoor, and Tudhoe;
* the District of Easington wards of Deaf Hill, Hutton Henry, Thornley, Wheatley Hill, and Wingate; and
* the Borough of Darlington wards of Heighington, Hurworth, Middleton St George, Sadberge, and Whessoe.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

*Spennymoor and Tudhoe transferred from [North West Durham](/wiki/North_West_Durham "North West Durham"); remainder of District of Sedgefield wards from [Durham](/wiki/City_of_Durham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "City of Durham (UK Parliament constituency)"); District of Easington wards from [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)"); and Borough of Darlington wards from [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Bishop Auckland (UK Parliament constituency)").*

### 1997–2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1997–2010")]

* The District of Sedgefield wards of Bishop Middleham, Broom, Chilton, Cornforth, Ferryhill, Fishburn, Middridge, Neville, New Trimdon and Trimdon Grange, Old Trimdon, Sedgefield, Shafto, Simpasture, West, and Woodham;
* the District of Easington wards of Deaf Hill, Hutton Henry, Thornley, Wheatley Hill, and Wingate; and
* the Borough of Darlington wards of Heighington, Hurworth, Middleton St George, Sadberge, and Whessoe.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

*Newton Aycliffe transferred in from Bishop Auckland in exchange for Spennymoor and Tudhoe.*

### 2010–2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010–2024")]

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,a,a,a,300x250.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Sedgefield_%28UK_Parliament_constituency%29&revid=1344162636&groups=_d8cda29267774d34f37d3f6a36b4685016ba094c&parser=legacy)

Map of 2010–2024 boundaries

* The Borough of Sedgefield wards of Bishop Middleham and Cornforth, Broom, Chilton, Ferryhill, Fishburn and Old Trimdon, Greenfield Middridge, Neville and Simpasture, New Trimdon and Trimdon Grange, Sedgefield, Shafto St Mary's, West, and Woodham;
* the District of Easington wards of Thornley and Wheatley Hill, and Wingate; and
* the Borough of Darlington wards of Heighington and Coniscliffe, Hurworth, Middleton St George, Sadberge, and Whessoe.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

*Minor changes only to reflect redrawing of local authority ward boundaries.*

In the [2009 structural changes to local government in England](/wiki/2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England "2009 structural changes to local government in England"), the local authority districts in Durham were abolished and replaced with a single [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"); however, this did affected the boundaries of the constituency.

### Constituency changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Constituency changes")]

The [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies") recommended that all wards in the Borough of Darlington should be removed from the seat, with [Shildon](/wiki/Shildon "Shildon") moving in from [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Bishop Auckland (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Coxhoe](/wiki/Coxhoe "Coxhoe") from [City of Durham](/wiki/City_of_Durham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "City of Durham (UK Parliament constituency)"). The reconfigured seat would be renamed "Newton Aycliffe and Sedgefield".

## Political history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Political history")]

From its recreation in 1983 until 27 June 2007, the Member of Parliament was [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair"), who led a successful campaign for his party to [win the 1997 general election in a landslide](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election") and thereafter served for ten years as prime minister, leading the campaigns at two subsequent general elections. Blair was the first Prime Minister to lead the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") to three consecutive victories. He resigned as the Member of Parliament for Sedgefield on the same day as he resigned as prime minister, which triggered a [by-election](/wiki/2007_Sedgefield_by-election "2007 Sedgefield by-election").[[10]](#cite_note-11)

At the by-election on 19 July 2007, the official Labour Party candidate [Phil Wilson](/wiki/Phil_Wilson,_Baron_Wilson_of_Sedgefield "Phil Wilson, Baron Wilson of Sedgefield") was elected on a reduced majority which in national terms is [safe](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat") instead of [marginal](/wiki/Marginal_seat "Marginal seat"). While Wilson had never came close to the enormous majorities held by Blair during his tenure as MP and only secured an absolute majority of the vote for the first time at the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), he consistently had majorities of over 6,000 votes in every election at which he had stood.

At the 2019 election, the Conservatives' candidate [Paul Howell](/wiki/Paul_Howell_(MP) "Paul Howell (MP)") defeated Wilson with a majority of 4,513 and a swing of 12.8%. Sedgefield was one of the net gain of 48 seats in England by the Conservatives, as well as being considered part of the so-called "[Red Wall](/wiki/Red_wall_(British_politics) "Red wall (British politics)")".

## Constituency profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedgefield_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Constituency profile")]

Sedgefield has a long [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") history (extracting [coal](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining"), [fluorspar](/wiki/Fluorspar "Fluorspar") and [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore")) and once had a very strong affiliation to the Labour Party, with nearly monolithic support in parts of the constituency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The area contains a mixture of former coal country in the area around [Trimdon](/wiki/Trimdon "Trimdon") and more industrial areas around the new town of [Newton Aycliffe](/wiki/Newton_Aycliffe "Newton Aycliffe"). The construction of a new [Hitachi factory](/wiki/Hitachi_Newton_Aycliffe "Hitachi Newton Aycliffe") created 730 jobs in the town.[[11]](#cite_note-12) There are also more prosperous parts of the constituency that form the bulk of the Conservative vote – for example, the ancient [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") of [Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield "Sedgefield") itself, with a charter dating back to 1312. The outer suburbs of [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington") are also relatively wealthy, as well as [Hurworth-on-Tees](/wiki/Hurworth-on-Tees "Hurworth-on-Tees"), where unemployment stands at just 1.0%.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

In statistics

The constituency consists of Census Output Areas of two local government districts with similar characteristics: a working population whose average income is lower than the national average and close-to-average reliance upon [social housing](/wiki/Social_housing "Social housing").[[13]](#cite_note-14) At the end of 2012 the unemployment rate in the constituency stood as 5.0% of the population claiming Jobseekers' Allowance, compared to the regional average of 5.5%.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

The local authority contributing to the bulk of the seat has a middling 27.2% of its population without a car, a high 27.5% of the population without qualifications and a medium 21.5% with level 4 qualifications or above. Darlington has 28% of its population without a car, 24.8% of the population without qualifications and a medium 23.7% with level 4 qualifications or above.

In terms of tenure 65.8% of County Durham homes and 64.9% of Darlington homes are owned outright or on a mortgage as at the 2011 census.[[15]](#cite_note-16)
